coredata.dc:title: Naturally derived materials to enhance the membrane properties in (waste)water treatment applications - Mechanisms, scale-up challenges and economic considerations
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Yongtao
Kamali, Mohammadreza
Al-Salem, S.M.
Rossi, Barbara
Appels, Lise
Dewil, Raf
coredata.dc:description: Membrane separation technologies are currently considered among the leading technologies for the treatment of polluted (waste)water in terms of treatment efficiency and ease of implementation. However, their wider applications, especially for real wastewater treatment, have been restricted by some bottlenecks, including rapid fouling, low stability, and high production costs. Recently, naturally derived materials including carbonaceous materials (e.g., biochar, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene and graphene oxide, and carbon quantum dots), clay (e.g., bentonite, kaolinite, and zeolite), and natural polymers (e.g., cellulose and chitin) have been employed for the modification of membrane structures to improve the properties required for efficient (waste)water treatment (e.g., water permeability, hydrophilicity, antifouling) and performance (e.g., rejection efficiency). The present review aims to summarize and discuss the literature available on the application of such naturally derived materials and their applicability for the large-scale fabrication of (waste)water filtration membranes. The existing challenges, scientific gaps, economic considerations, and regional development of membrane technologies have also been discussed, and recommendations have been provided for future studies to develop sustainable membrane structures, leading to a decrease in the overall treatment costs and enhancing the quality of treatment of effluents from industrial and nonindustrial sources.

head.ce:title: Naturally derived materials to enhance the membrane properties in (waste)water treatment applications - Mechanisms, scale-up challenges and economic considerations

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Membrane separation technologies are currently considered among the leading technologies for the treatment of polluted (waste)water in terms of treatment efficiency and ease of implementation. However, their wider applications, especially for real wastewater treatment, have been restricted by some bottlenecks, including rapid fouling, low stability, and high production costs. Recently, naturally derived materials including carbonaceous materials (e.g., biochar, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene and graphene oxide, and carbon quantum dots), clay (e.g., bentonite, kaolinite, and zeolite), and natural polymers (e.g., cellulose and chitin) have been employed for the modification of membrane structures to improve the properties required for efficient (waste)water treatment (e.g., water permeability, hydrophilicity, antifouling) and performance (e.g., rejection efficiency). The present review aims to summarize and discuss the literature available on the application of such naturally derived materials and their applicability for the large-scale fabrication of (waste)water filtration membranes. The existing challenges, scientific gaps, economic considerations, and regional development of membrane technologies have also been discussed, and recommendations have been provided for future studies to develop sustainable membrane structures, leading to a decrease in the overall treatment costs and enhancing the quality of treatment of effluents from industrial and nonindustrial sources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, various biological and physicochemical technologies have been developed and implemented in (waste)water treatment applications to satisfy the requirements of green growth and sustainable development [1–3]. Among them, membrane separation technologies with different configurations, including nanofiltration [4], ultrafiltration [5], microfiltration [6], reverse osmosis [7], and forward osmosis [8], have been employed as leading techniques for the separation of pollutants from wastewater originating from various industrial and nonindustrial sources [9]. However, there are currently issues related to the performance of membrane technologies, such as the formation of the cake layer and rapid fouling of the membrane [10], the trade-off problem between high water permeability and high rejection efficiency [11], and the low stability and high production costs of the membrane [12,13]. In this regard, various methods have been reported to improve the performance of membranes, such as the introduction of surface functional groups [14], changing the surface charges of the membrane [15], and granting hydrophilic properties to the membrane [16].

Recently, the introduction of naturally derived materials into membranes has been confirmed to not only improve the performance of the membrane but also reduce production costs [17]. Naturally derived materials normally refer to the feedstocks for the synthesis of various materials (e.g., carbonaceous materials and clay-based materials) that are obtained directly from natural resources such as biomass (e.g., wood and plants), clay (e.g., bentonite), and natural polymers (e.g., natural rubber and cellulose) [18]. These materials can be incorporated into membrane structures using existing fabrication methods (e.g., phase inversion, interfacial polymerization, and vacuum filtration processes) without the need for any specific techniques developed to this end. According to the literature, carbonaceous materials (e.g., biochar, activated carbon, and carbon nanotubes) are inexpensive and abundant, which makes them good candidates for the modification of membranes. They are also characterized by the presence of surface functional groups, high specific surface area, and high porosity, which can not only increase the water permeability but also increase the hydrophilicity of the membrane, resulting in the improved antifouling performance of the membrane [19–22]. Clay-based materials can also endow membranes with unique properties, such as high mechanical and chemical stabilities and high water permeability. Such membranes can also represent an outstanding resistance to foulants. Hence, less fouling can be expected, which can be cured easily and without any damage to the membrane structure [23–25]. Natural polymers are also considered green and biodegradable materials that can be used to improve the mentioned properties of membranes [26,27].

Despite the effectiveness of such materials for improving membrane properties, no review has yet been published concerning these issues. Hence, assessing the applicability of such naturally derived materials for the modification of membranes for efficient (waste)water treatment applications is highly welcome. To fill these gaps, this review has aimed at summarizing and discussing the literature available on the application of naturally derived materials, including carbonaceous materials (e.g., biochar and activated carbon), clay-based materials (e.g., bentonite and kaolinite), and natural polymers such as cellulose and chitin, to enhance the properties of membranes, such as hydrophilicity, surface roughness, water permeability, rejection efficiency, and mechanical strength. The effects of such modifications on the membrane performance in terms of treatment efficiency, antifouling performance, etc., have also been discussed critically. The future perspective of the application of such technologies in real wastewater treatment applications has also been considered in the present manuscript, and recommendations have been provided for future studies in this field.

Carbonaceous feedstocks have attracted increasing attention in recent decades for the fabrication of various structures to be used in (waste)water treatment, such as adsorbents [28], electrodes [29], and catalysts [30]. This is mainly because they are abundant and inexpensive, and their sequestration can aid in the mitigation of current environmental issues such as climate change and global warming [31]. In this regard, a wide range of carbon-based materials, such as biochar, activated carbon, graphene, and carbon quantum dots, have been used for the fabrication of carbonaceous membranes. This section critically reviews and discusses the technological advances in the fabrication of these types of membrane structures for (waste)water filtration purposes.

Biochar is a carbon-rich porous solid product that is normally obtained by pyrolysis of biomass at temperatures above 250 °C under inert atmospheres (e.g., nitrogen) [32]. Biochar can be produced from a range of abundant and inexpensive feedstocks, such as agricultural wastes, sludge from wastewater treatment plants, and woody materials [33]. This, together with the special properties of biochar, such as high porosity and specific surface area, makes it a competitive candidate for (waste)water treatment applications [34]. Zhang et al. [35] prepared biochar with an ultrafine particle size (average particle size D50 = 0.623 μm) from crayfish shells by a combination of pyrolysis and ball-milling methods. The prepared biochar was used to prepare a membrane with polydopamine (as the adhesion agent) for the treatment of pharmaceutical effluents. The membrane with a thickness of 1.4 μm displayed high rejection rates for chlortetracycline (92 %), tetracycline (90 %), ciprofloxacin (82 %), and oxytetracycline (91 %). This result can be attributed to the fact that ball-milled biochar can increase the hydrophilicity and permeability of the prepared membrane, which proved that ball-milled biochar can open the water channel and improve the solvent transport of the membranes [36]. It has been proven that ball-milled biochar exhibits a larger specific surface area, higher porosity, and smaller particle size than pristine biochar. This is because the large particles are crushed under the ball milling process into ultrafine particles. This process can also result in opening the abundant closed pore networks in biochar. In addition, the enhancement in the performance of the membrane by the addition of biochar can be due to the promoted adsorption capacity of the membrane for specific pollutants. For instance, the presence of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl groups in biochar can increase the adsorption capacity of phenolic pollutants due to the increased electrostatic effect between biochar and these compounds [37]. As another example, Mohammadi et al. [38] prepared a new type of biochar from Rosmarinus officinalis leaves with a high adsorption capacity for phosphate (78.24 mg/g) using a pyrolysis process. Various mechanisms, including ion exchange, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonding between the biochar and phosphate, were involved in the promoted adsorption capacity of the prepared materials. The membrane fabricated from 15 wt% biochar and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) also inherited the highest phosphate removal efficiency (approximately 50 % at 5 min contact time) compared to membranes with 5 % and 10 % wt% biochar (24 % and 19 %, respectively). This may be ascribed to the presence of more biochar, which can provide more channels for ion transport and reduce ion transmembrane resistance, so ions can pass through the membrane more easily [39].

The pyrolysis temperature is among the most important operating parameters that can considerably affect the yield and properties of biochar, such as the specific surface area, porosity and carbon content. For instance, Ghaffar et al. [40] prepared two different woody biochars at pyrolysis temperatures of 300 °C (BC-300) and 700 °C (BC-700) and then used the prepared materials to fabricate membranes through a thermal phase inversion process. The membrane prepared by BC-700 demonstrated a higher water flux (6869 > 5411 L/m2 h) and higher adsorption capacity for rhodamine B (RhB, 170 > 96 mg/g), which could be attributed to the higher specific surface area and pore volume of BC-700 compared to those of BC-300 (e.g., 55.30 > 29.73 m2/g and 0.048 > 0.026 cm3/g, respectively). As a rule, a higher specific surface area and porosity can provide more active sites to promote physicochemical reactions, resulting in the removal of pollutants from the medium [41]. It has also been confirmed that complete biomass carbonization occurs at temperatures above 550 °C. By elevating the temperature, more volatile organic compounds are generated, leading to the formation of porous structures with a large specific surface area [42].

As discussed earlier, the adsorption of pollutants by biochar in the structure of the membranes is the dominant mechanism for the removal of pollutants. Different mechanisms, including electrostatic interactions, ion exchange, H-bonding, and hydrophobic interactions, are involved in the adsorption process. The electrostatic interactions between the pollutants and adsorbent are highly dependent on their surface charges; hence, the solution pH and surface charges of membranes play key roles in their attractive or repulsive forces [43]. In addition, ion exchange is basically classified into cation exchange for the adsorption of positively charged ions and anion exchange for the removal of negatively charged ions in the medium [44]. H-bonding is another main adsorption mechanism based on the electrostatic attraction between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a very electronegative atom (i.e., O, N) [45]. Hydrophobic interactions have also been reported as one of the main mechanisms in the adsorption process. It normally occurs between nonpolar molecules (pollutants and adsorbents), resulting in the aggregation and removal of pollutants in the medium [46]. However, the adsorption process can potentially lead to fouling, which can restrict the regeneration of the membrane [47]. To address this issue, the application of modified biochar as a catalyst to promote the advanced oxidation of organic pollutants has attracted enormous interest. Huang et al. [48] fabricated a biochar/geopolymer composite membrane through in situ synchronous carbonation and self-activation processes from alkali lignin and geopolymer composites. The prepared membrane with a large surface area of 37 m2/g was rich in functional groups such as C-OH, CO, and O=C-O. The membrane (0.15 g, 1 mL H2O2) showed almost 100 % removal of tetracycline with an initial concentration of 50 mg/L. The excellent performance of the membrane can be associated with two aspects. First, the phenolic-OH, ketone, and quinone functional groups can transfer electrons to H2O2 and promote the generation of OH, which is the main reactive species for the degradation of tetracycline [49]. Second, the porous structure and large specific surface area of biochar make the catalyst in close contact with tetracycline molecules, so accelerating electron transfer in the redox reaction process can inhibit the decay of free radicals, resulting in the effective removal of tetracycline [50].

Activated carbon (AC) is a porous carbonaceous material that normally has a large specific surface area and a high carbon content (>90 %) [51]. AC is usually rich in functional groups such as carboxyl, phenol, lactone, hydroxide, and quinone [52]. For instance, Zhang et al. [53] fabricated a calcium alginate/AC membrane through vacuum freeze-drying and crosslinking processes. The prepared membrane with 30 % AC demonstrated a higher removal efficiency of 94 % for methylene blue (MB) compared to the membrane without AC (79 %). This can be ascribed to the fact that the addition of AC leads to a decrease in the internal pore size, which further enhances the prevention of MB molecules. At the same time, AC provides more active sites diffused into the inner cavities of the membrane, which also improves the filtration performance of the membrane.

It has been well documented in the literature that AC can be considered a good candidate for the adsorption of various organic ([54]) and inorganic ([55]) compounds. The membranes fabricated from AC can not only adsorb pollutants but also separate them based on the pore size of the membrane. Yang et al., [56] prepared a wood AC membrane by using structure exfoliation and reconstruction processes. The membrane not only demonstrated a high adsorption capacity for MB (252.4 mg/g) but also displayed an excellent separation performance for Ag nanoparticles (10 nm, 96 %) and graphene oxide quantum dots (5-10 nm, 80.61 %). These results can be attributed to the narrow pore distribution (5-10 nm) of the membrane and the high water flux that can be provided by the membrane (2316 L/m2hbar).

The application of ACs with high specific surface areas can also enhance the adsorption capacity of the membrane, which, together with the filtration performance, can make the membrane very efficient for the removal of environmental contaminants. For instance, Manoukian et al. [57] fabricated a polysulfone-activated carbon membrane through a casting technique using highly uniform carbon spheres with a very high surface area of 1792 m2/g as the raw materials. The membrane showed an excellent MB rejection efficiency of almost 100 %, which can be ascribed to the synergistic effect of adsorption and nanofiltration mechanisms. They concluded that various mechanisms, including π–π electron-donor-acceptor interactions, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions, can contribute to the adsorption of MB to the membrane surface. Additionally, the nanofiltration process was highly influenced by the pore size distribution (mean pore size of 0.83 nm) and water flux (1.1 Lm−2 h−1) of the membrane. However, the high adsorption capacity of the membrane may lead to rapid fouling and the need for periodic cleaning/replacement of the membrane, which can considerably increase the treatment costs. To address this issue, some measures can be recommended, for instance, incorporating catalytic materials on the surface of the membrane (such as CuO nanomaterials), which can activate oxidation agents such as persulfate and persulfate to produce highly efficient oxidative radicals (Eq. (1)), which can attach and decompose the adsorbed organic compounds on the surface and within the pores of the membrane [58].

Additionally, such structures may be damaged during long-term use, and oxidation agents need to be added into the system at regular intervals. In this regard, the modification of the membrane, including improving the hydrophilicity and surface charges, is considered an efficient and facile way to enhance the antifouling behavior of membranes. This can be attributed to the fact that the membrane with a high hydrophilicity tends to adsorb water molecules, which then form a hydration layer between the membrane surface and the contaminants, which can reduce the interaction of the membrane and the pollutants and inhibit the adsorption of organic pollutants on the membrane surface [59]. In addition, the opposing surface charges between the membrane and contaminants can reduce their adsorption due to the electrostatic repulsion that occurs under a charge difference. There are also reports available in the literature indicating that the application of AC in the membrane structure can enhance the antifouling behavior, which can guarantee the long-term performance of membrane units. Kallem et al. [60] synthesized hydroxyapatite-decorated orange peel activated carbon nanocomposite membranes for ultrafiltration applications. As shown in Fig. 1a, the membranes with 4 % nanocomposites (wt%, PES-4) displayed the highest flux recovery ratio and the lowest adsorption behavior for humic acid (HA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA). In Fig. 1b, the value of reversible fouling (Rr) increased with increasing membrane nanocomposite content, indicating that the addition of the nanocomposite can improve the antifouling performance of the membrane. In fact, high Rr values suggest that the pollutants are deposited on the membrane surface and can be separated by physical cleaning. The membrane without the nanocomposite (PES-0) showed the highest value of irreversible fouling (Rir), indicating the strong adsorption of pollutants on the membrane surface or blockage of membrane pores. Under these conditions, efficient chemical cleaning is required for the removal of pollutants from the pores of the membranes. It can be concluded that the PES-4 membrane showed excellent antifouling performance because of the presence of nanocomposites (Fig. 1c), which enhanced the hydrophilicity of the membrane, and the formation of a hydrophilic layer inhibited the adsorption and desorption process for HA and BSA [61]. Furthermore, the negatively charged functional groups such as -COOH and -OH− of the composites further reduced the adsorption capacity for HA and BSA due to the effect of electrostatic repulsion [62].

There are also other attempts in the literature to enhance the fouling resistance of the membrane through the combination of inorganic materials and AC to prepare composite membrane structures. For instance, Zareei et al. [64] synthesized cobalt ferrite/activated carbon composite nanoparticles using a facile chemical precipitation method and then used them for the fabrication of a polyethersulfone-based composite nanofiltration membrane via a phase inversion method. They found that the membrane with 1 wt% nanocomposite can reduce the porosity of the pristine membrane (from 70 % to 68 %), which can be attributed to the fact that the addition of excess nanoparticles may increase the viscosity of the casting solution along with pore filling, thereby reducing the porosity of the membrane [65]. Nonetheless, the membrane demonstrated higher hydrophilicity and lower water flux than the pristine membrane. The abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of composite nanoparticles can improve the surface hydrophilicity, but the excess nanoparticles decrease the porosity and pore size and then lead to a decrease in water flux. The membrane with 1 wt% nanoparticles displayed high rejections of 86 %, 90 %, and 97 % for Pb2+, Ni2+, and Cu2+, respectively, which can be related to the Donnan exclusion effect with negative charges of the membrane surface and negative ions. Additionally, nanomaterials provide abundant adsorption sites, and the reduction in pore size is conducive to the rejection of ions. It has also been confirmed that a membrane with high surface hydrophilicity has a stronger ability to adsorb water molecules, and the rejection ability of the membrane can be improved by reducing concentration polarization and pollutant precipitation [66].

The incorporation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) or multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) into the structure of membranes for (waste)water treatment applications has received much attention in recent years. The fabricated membranes normally have high mechanical strength, excellent electron affinity, and high flexibility for applying the required modifications [67]. However, carbon nanotubes are normally generally expensive to prepare by using conventional fabrication techniques. Recently, some novel technologies have been developed for the synthesis of such structures with a low fabrication cost. As an example, Baghel et al. [68] synthesized CNTs from Prosopis juliflora biochar through microwave irradiation, which is an inexpensive and efficient synthesis method. During the synthesis process, the temperature of the mixture (biochar and ferrocene) rises dramatically within seconds under microwave irradiation. This is because biochar has excellent microwave adsorption capacity and can convert energy to heat quickly. As a result, free carbon atoms are generated from the decomposition of biochar. Furthermore, ferrocene decomposes into free iron atoms (Fe) and cyclopentadienyl (C5H5) under the energy input from microwave irradiation. Subsequently, iron nanoparticles are formed on the surface of biochar due to the condensation and aggregation of free iron atoms. Finally, iron nanoparticles as catalysts can react with free carbon atoms to produce CNTs under microwave irradiation conditions. In another study, Hidalgo et al. [69] fabricated high-quality carbon nanotubes by using biochar and ferrocene as the precursor through a microwave irradiation process (Fig. 2 ). They also observed that biochar with a higher aromaticity resulted in a less stable structure and was more conducive to CNT growth. In addition, the ash of the carbon source can promote CNT growth due to the catalytic activity of its alkaline metals.

Hence, carbon nanotubes can be considered a candidate for the fabrication of membranes from naturally derived materials. In addition, SWCNTs consist of a single graphene cylinder with a typical diameter of 0.7-2 nm, while MWCNTs normally consist of several concentric graphene cylinders with a diameter of 30 nm [70]. In general, small SWCNTs easily aggregate and form weak interactions with the polymer matrix due to the strong interactions of intertube CC van der Waals forces [71]. Hence, modification of SWCNTs with hydrophilic functional groups, such as carboxyl groups, is considered to be an effective method to enhance their applicability in membrane structures. Yang et al. [72] fabricated a novel nanofiltration membrane using carboxylate single-walled carbon nanotubes (COOH-SWCNTs) embedded into an ultrathin polyamide layer. The membrane displayed an excellent water flux of 23 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and a high salt rejection of Na2SO4 solution (95 %). Several aspects can explain the high performance of this type of membrane, including a) COOH-SWCNTs can increase the membrane roughness, resulting in an increase in the effective area for water permeation, b) the carboxyl group of SWCNTs can improve the membrane hydrophilicity, and c) SWCNTs provide more water channels to enhance the water flux, which is beneficial to improving the salt rejection performance of the membrane.

In addition to carboxyl-functionalized CNTs, amine-functionalized structures can also increase the hydrophilicity and permeability of membranes. For instance, Vatanpour et al. [73] developed a reverse osmosis membrane using the interfacial polymerization of m-phenylenediamine and trimethyl chloride monomers and modified it with amine-functionalized MWCNTs. They observed that the roughness of the membrane decreased obviously by incorporating 0.001 or 0.002 wt% NH2-MWCNTs, which might be because hydrophilic NH2-MWCNTs can promote interfacial polymerization by increasing the miscibility between aqueous and organic phases. In addition, the hydrogen bonding between the polyamide layer and the functional groups of NH2-MWCNTs can also result in a decrease in the roughness of the membrane [74]. According to the observations of Vatanpour et al. [75], the membrane with 0.002 wt% NH2-MWCNTs displayed high NaCl rejection (96 %) and antifouling properties (flux recovery ratio of 86 %), which may be due to the increased water permeability (42, 58 L·m−2·h−1 for the membrane with 0, 0.002 wt% NH2-MWCNTs, respectively) and reduced roughness. However, a further increase in the NH2-MWCNT content may lead to an increase in the membrane roughness considering that excess NH2-MWCNTs can limit the dispersibility and agglomeration of NH2-MWCNTs during the polymerization process [76].

The effects of the presence of different functional groups in CNTs on membrane performance have also been reported by Xue et al. [77]. They used three modified MWCNTs with carboxyl (MWCNT-COOH), hydroxyl (MWCNT-OH), and amino groups (MWCNT-NH) to fabricate the nanofiltration membrane via an interfacial polymerization method to generate a polyamide layer. The results revealed that the MWCNT-OH membrane had the smallest nodular structures among the pristine and modified membranes, which might be due to the presence of amino groups of polyamide and the hydroxyl group of MWCNT-OH reacting with the acyl chloride group of trimesoyl chloride to form chain and ester bonds. The hydrophilicity of the membrane followed the order of MWCNT-COOH > MWCNT-OH > MWCNT-NH > pristine MWCNT (contact angles of 12.5°, 14.5°, 16°, and 21°), which can be attributed to the fact that the carboxyl group has the strongest hydrophilicity. The MWCNT-OH membrane had the highest water flux of 41 Lm−2h−1bar−1 and the highest Na2SO4 rejection of 97.6 % induced by the synergistic effect of MWCNT-OH and the amino group of polyamide.

Despite the enhanced properties (e.g., water permeability, rejection efficiency) of functional group-modified CNT-based membranes, the membrane surface charges are also a key factor determining the separation of pollutants. The difference in the zeta potential of the membrane with the charges of the pollutants can result in their rejection and prevention of their adhesion to the surface of the membrane, which can lead to the formation of the cake layer and to the fouling of the pores. There are some reports in the literature evidencing such statements. For instance, Zhao et al. [78] fabricated a positively charged nanofiltration membrane by uniformly embedding poly(dopamine)-modified multiwall carbon nanotubes (PDA-MWCNTs) in polyamide thin-film composite membranes. The PDA-MWCNT membrane presented a high pure water permeability of 15 Lm−2h−1bar−1 as well as a high salt rejection of over 90 % for Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, and Ca2+. The PDA-MWCNT membrane had a zeta potential of 4.1 ± 1.4 mV at pH 6, so the positive charges on the surface of the PDA-MWCNT membrane under such pH conditions promoted the repulsion of the positively charged ions (e.g., Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+) (Fig. 3a-b). In such cases, there is a need for information about the zero point charge (ZPC) of the membranes to predict the ions that can be rejected under various pH conditions [79]. For instance, Venkatesh et al. [80] prepared diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA)-functionalized MWCNT/TiO2 nanofiber membranes with excellent hydrophilicity (contact angle of 27°) and a ZPC of 4.2. They have discussed that because the surface of the membrane is negatively charged at pH values over 4.2, a highly effective separation for anionic compounds can be expected. They reported 98 % rejection of the anionic engine oil and water mixture, and they mentioned that in addition to the surface charge of the membrane, the high performance of the system can also be attributed to the small average pore size of the membrane (7.3 nm), which was smaller than the average emulsion diameter of anionic engine oil (3.4 um), so most of the engine oil can be separated by the membrane (Fig. 3c).

Graphene is a two-dimensional material with a single ultrathin layer of sp2-bonded carbon packed into a honeycomb pattern [82]. This material has unique properties, including a large specific surface area, superior thermal conductivity, and remarkable excellent charge carrier mobility [83]. Graphene oxide (GO) is an oxidized derivative of graphene with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups, with properties such as a large specific surface area and chemical and mechanical stability [84]. Compared to graphene, GO has a higher hydrophilicity due to the oxygen-containing groups. Graphene- and GO-based membrane structures have been widely implemented in (waste)water treatment due to their unique properties, such as high rejection, enhanced water permeability, and considerable fouling resistance. Additionally, several methods have been attempted to reduce the cost and facilitate the commercialization of GO-based materials. For instance, Ranjan et al. [85] implemented a low-cost and non-explosive process for the synthesis of GO. There was also no need for expensive membranes to filter the residual carbonaceous and metallic compounds because of the precise control of the reaction parameters, including temperature and time. In addition, a pre-cooling system was used during the oxidation process. This can alleviate the cost of GO and eliminate the explosive nature of the intermediate reaction steps.

The addition of graphene-based materials can affect the surface properties of the membranes. Roughness is among the most important characteristics of the membrane, and in principle, increasing roughness can result in promoting both water permeability and fouling [86,87]. The recent literature has indicated that the addition of graphene-based materials can influence the roughness of membrane structures; hence, there is a need to determine the optimum dosage of nanomaterials that results in the best performance of the membrane. Yin et al. [88] fabricated a polyamide thin-film nanocomposite membrane containing GO nanosheets using an in situ interfacial polymerization process. They indicated that the roughness of the membrane decreased as the GO dosage increased from 0 to 0.015 %, while the roughness of the membrane increased again at a GO concentration of 0.02 %. Under low loading rates of GO, the membrane roughness decreases, forming a smooth surface due to the low electrostatic interaction and good compatibility with the membrane matrix. The excess nanomaterials can form surface-located clusters due to the aggregation of nanomaterials, so the roughness of the membrane increased again under the high loading of carbon materials. Similar behaviors have been reported in other kinds of literature [89]. The membrane with GO demonstrated a higher water permeability than the membrane without GO (60 L/m2h for 0.015 GO, 39 L/m2h without GO), while the salt rejection for NaCl and Na2SO4 slightly decreased from 96 % to 94 % and 98 % to 97 %, respectively. Such a slight decrease in salt rejection can be because the abundant oxygen functional groups can prevent water transport by hydrogen bond interactions [90]. In addition, GO easily expands and redisperses in water due to hydration on the GO plane, causing swelling of oxygen-containing groups [91]. Hence, the reduction of GO to prepare reduced graphene oxide (RGO) is an ideal way to solve the problem of GO membrane swelling. Yu et al. [92] fabricated a series of RGO membranes with various degrees of GO reduction controlled by a UV irradiation reduction process. They observed that the color of the membrane changed from light yellow to dark brown, and the contact angle of the membrane increased from 52°, 65°, 70°, and 73° with increasing UV irradiation time (0, 6, 12, 18 h). These results revealed that some oxygen-containing groups disappeared in the structure of GO due to the effect of UV reduction, and the characterization results proved that the oxygen content of the membrane decreased from 39 % (0 h) to 32 % (6 h), 26 % (12h), and 20 % (18 h). Additionally, the D-spacing of the GO adjacent layers decreased from 12.44 Å (0 h) to 12.24 Å (6 h), 12.15 Å (12 h), and 12.06 Å (18 h), which can be ascribed to the fact that the interlayer spacing of GO becomes close by π-π interactions due to the decrease in oxygen-containing groups between GO layers [93]. The membrane with 6 h UV irradiation displayed the highest water flux and rejection efficiency for RhB (38 Lm−2h−1bar−1, 99 %) compared to the other membranes (0 h, 14 Lm−2h−1bar−1, 92 %; 12 h, 19 Lm−2h−1bar−1, 87 %; 18 h, 12 Lm−2h−1bar−1, 68 %). The increased water flux and RhB rejection efficiency of the membrane (6 h) can be attributed to the decrease in the oxygen-containing groups leading to the improvement in the hydrophobicity and increasing the sp2 domains, frictionless water channels, and microgaps between adjacent GO layers. However, the lack of hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups and small D-spacing of the GO adjacent layers in the samples prepared under an increased reduction time (from 6 to 12 and 18 h) resulted in a decline in the water permeability of the membrane [94].

Although the GO membrane has been considered a promising candidate for (waste)water treatment, its wider application is seriously limited by the poor permeability of organic solvents and the instability of an aqueous solution [95]. To overcome this issue, some strategies have been proposed in the literature, such as the combination of GO and other nanomaterials with high stability and high organic solvent permeability. For instance, MXene is a 2D material with a chemical formula of M
n+1 X
n
T
x , where n stands for 1, 2, or 3, M represents an early transition metal, X refers to carbon and/or nitrogen, and T refers to the surface group (OH, O, or F). In general, MXene-fabricated membranes exhibit high water permeability due to their laminar structure and hydrophilic properties [96]. Liu et al. [97] prepared a GO/MXene (Ti3C2Tx) composite membrane using a commercial mixed cellulose ester membrane (0.45 μm) as the supporting layer through a filtration process. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) characterization revealed that the GO/MXene membrane can demonstrate a lower stretching vibration of -OH and -COOH and a lower intensity peak of CO (XPS) compared to the pure GO membrane, suggesting that the oxygen-containing functional groups of the membrane decreased because MXene contains a small quantity of terminating functional groups [98]. Furthermore, the GO/MXene membrane showed an increased D-spacing of adjacent layers, which can be attributed to the fact that the D-spacing of adjacent layers for pure GO and MXene were approximately 7 Å and 15 Å from the XRD calculation, respectively. The GO/MXene membrane displayed an excellent water permeation of 72 Lm−2h−1bar−1, which is 10 times higher than that of the pure GO membrane. The GO/MXene membrane also had an excellent rejection efficiency of almost 100 % for chrysoidine G, neutral red, MB, crystal violet, brilliants blue, HA, and BSA. These excellent performances of the membrane can be explained as follows: the hydrogen bond interaction is reduced between water molecules and oxygen-containing functional groups owing to the decrease in oxygen-containing functional groups [99]. In addition, the addition of MXene reduces the π-π interaction between GO nanosheets and the increment of the D-spacing adjacent layers, resulting in a high water flux [100].

As discussed before, various attempts have been made to solve the fouling problem of membrane structures to minimize the operating costs and to promote them for real applications. The addition of carbonaceous catalysts to membrane structures to activate oxidation agents is a viable method to remove foulants, which can cause clogging of membranes. Recently, the strategy of integrating GO-based photocatalysts into the structure of the membrane has been proposed to degrade organic pollutants. Materials such as g-C3N4/RGO [101] and TiO2/GO [102] are some examples in this regard. Xie et al. [103] synthesized an Fe(III)-based metal-organic framework (M88A) nanostructure using a hydrothermal method and then used M88A to fabricate a GO/M88A membrane through a vacuum-filtration process. The GO/M88A membrane demonstrated almost 100 % rejection efficiency for MB in the dark and visible light in the presence of H2O2. However, the MB flux of the membrane decreased under dark conditions (Fig. 4a) due to MB molecules accumulating on the surface or pores of the membrane, while the flux increased gradually under visible light, suggesting that the MB molecules of the membrane surface or pores were degraded during the photo-Fenton process. They found that the mechanism of the self-cleaning membrane was mainly dominated by various active species, such as OH, O2 −, and h+ (Fig. 4b). Under visible light, M88A can be excited to generate e− and h+, and e− can not only promote the decomposition of H2O2 to generate OH but also react with O2 to generate O2 −. Additionally, the GO/M88A membrane displayed a higher pure water flux (50 Lm−2h−1bar−1) than the GO membrane (14 Lm−2h−1bar−1). This can be ascribed to the fact that M88A can enrich the channels of the membrane for water molecule transport [104]. Moreover, M88A can also increase the hydrophilicity of the membrane, which can reduce the resistance of water molecules through the membrane, increasing water flux [105]. Research in this area can provide new insight into using visible light to overcome the fouling issue of membranes, which can satisfy sustainability considerations.

In addition, GO and GO-based materials normally demonstrate strong antimicrobial properties against various gram-positive/-negative bacteria and phytopathogens [107]. Hence, GO and GO-based membranes can display significant potential for bacterial inactivation in wastewater treatment. For instance, Duan et al. [108] fabricated a GO-immobilized enzyme (lysozyme)/polyethersulfone mixed matrix membrane, and the membrane presented an effective antibacterial performance against Escherichia coli. This can be attributed to the fact that lysozyme with high protein activity can quickly catalyze the hydrolysis of bacterial cell walls. Additionally, GO can improve the antibacterial performance of the membrane by inducing oxidative stress and physical disruption of the cell membrane. Oxidative stress is controlled by reactive oxygen species (ROS), including 1O2, O2 ∙−, and ∙OH radicals produced from GO, and direct contact between bacteria and the extremely sharp edges of GO can lead to cell damage of the bacteria.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are generally defined as quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters of approximately 10 nm [109]. Although conventional methods normally lead to CQDs with high fabrication costs, there are reports of their fabrication from low-cost feedstock materials. For instance, Yang et al. [110] synthesized N-doped CQDs using Eichhornia crassipes (ECs) as precursors via a one-pot method. The solar cells fabricated from N-doped CQDs displayed outstanding optical properties and achieved a maximized solar-to-electrical conversion efficiency of 0.17 % with a good fill factor of 67 %. Hence, CQDs have attracted extensive attention in the fabrication of membranes for (waste)water treatment due to their inherent properties, such as ultrasmall sizes, good biocompatibility, and rich surface functional groups [111]. Gai et al. [112] fabricated an amino-CQD-modified hollow fiber membrane via the interfacial polymerization process. The membrane without CQDs showed a smooth polyamide layer with a nodular-like morphology, while with CQDs, it displayed a rough polyamide layer with valley and peak morphologies, suggesting that the addition of CQDs could increase the surface roughness of the membrane. This result can be attributed to the fact that CQDs can interfere with the crosslinking reaction between aqueous and organic phases, resulting in the formation of a less cross-linked polyamide layer during the interfacial polymerization process. Furthermore, the increased surface roughness of the membrane can be explained by the diffusion channels created by CQDs, which makes the membrane structure more amorphous [113]. The amino-CQD membrane demonstrated higher water permeability than the nonmodified membrane (5.5 > 3.9 Lm−2h−1bar−1), which can also be attributed to the water diffusion channels created in the membrane structure, as well as the presence of functional groups of CQDs, which can increase the hydrophilicity of the membrane [114].

It can be concluded that the functional groups of CQDs can significantly influence the properties of the membrane required for efficient (waste)water treatment. Hence, there have been efforts to include CQDs with different types of functional groups in the structure of the membranes. For instance, Sun et al. [115] synthesized three different types of functionalized CQDs with a) carboxyl (CCQD), b) amino (NCQD), and c) sulfonic acid groups (SCQD) and then used them for the fabrication of nanocomposite membranes via an interfacial polymerization process. The modified membranes presented similar hydrophilicity properties, but all were higher than those of the pristine membrane, suggesting that the carboxyl, amino, and sulfonic acid groups of the CQDs can all increase the hydrophilicity of the membrane, which is essential to mitigate membrane fouling. Although all the membranes presented negative surface charges (at a pH of approximately 6), the membranes modified with CCQDs and SCQDs presented more negative surface charges (-16 mV and -18 mV, respectively) compared to that of NCQDs (-7 mV). This can be related to the anionic carboxyl or sulfonate groups of the CQDs, which are responsible for the negative charges on the surface of the membrane. In addition, the membrane with CCQDs and SCQDs displayed better antifouling properties for BSA than the membrane with NCQDs or without CQDs. This can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsion between the membrane surface and BSA because BSA is negatively charged at pH values above 7 [116].

Co-incorporation of CQDs and inorganic nanomaterials can also enhance the properties of the membrane, such as water permeability and antifouling performance. Feng et al. [117] found that the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 (metal-organic framework (MOF) nanomaterial) membrane can display higher water permeability (359 L/m2h) than the CQD membrane (273 L/m2h), UiO-66-NH2 membrane (260 L/m2h), and pristine membrane (200 L/m2h). This can be ascribed to the synergistic effect of the CQDs and the MOF, which can increase the porosity and hydrophilicity of the membrane. The excellent hydrophilicity of the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 nanocomposite (contact angle of 65°) resulted in high antifouling performance for BSA (flux recovery ratio of 70 %).

In addition to the materials discussed in the previous sections, there are other types of carbon structures, such as diamond and graphite, which have rarely been used for membrane modification. Hence, this section discusses their wider applicability for improving the properties of the membranes.

Diamond is a unique carbonaceous structure formed by complete C-sp3 hybridization and tetrahedral bonding. This material is especially well known for its properties, such as high chemical stability, thermal conductivity, and high carrier mobility [118]. These properties can make the membranes modified by diamond good candidates for (waste)water treatment. The presence of functional groups on such membranes can potentially increase the hydrophilicity of the membranes, which is essential for efficient water filtration. For instance, Javadi et al. [119] fabricated a polyvinylpyrrolidone-modified nanodiamond membrane (P-ND) via a phase inversion process. The P-ND membrane displayed a higher water flux (122 L/m2h) than the pristine membrane (27 L/m2h), which can be related to the fact that the functional groups of P-ND (e.g., CO, CN) can increase the hydrophilicity of the membrane. Furthermore, the P-ND nanoparticles can increase the porosity of the membrane, which is also beneficial for increasing the water flux of the membrane. In a practical study, the pharmaceutical (waste)water flux of the P-ND membrane decreased slightly from 56 to 37 kg/m2h (<33 %) in the membrane bioreactor for 6 h, suggesting that the P-ND membrane had outstanding antifouling performance. This can be attributed to the excellent hydrophilicity and high water flux of the membrane. In addition, the membrane displayed antibacterial properties leading to a decrease in the adsorption of proteins on the membrane surface [120]. Furthermore, the excellent antibacterial properties can be attributed to the fact that diamond crystalline structures can mechanically destroy the membranes of the cells [121].

Graphite is another widely available carbonaceous material with a stacked planar sp2-hybridized C6 ring structure, which leads to its good thermal and electrical conductivity. Furthermore, graphite is known for its high regular stiffness and strength [122]. All these characteristics make graphite an option for the modification of membranes. For instance, Jiang et al. [123] synthesized a TiO2/graphite felt (TiO2@GF) membrane as a photoanode to combine UV irradiation with electro-Fenton catalysis for the treatment of florfenicol (Fig. 5a). Almost complete degradation of florfenicol was obtained in the photoelectrochemical/electro-Fenton synchronous reaction of the sequential filtration system, which can be attributed to some aspects, including a) the UV light can activate titanium dioxide in the TiO2/graphite felt membrane to generate radicals (e.g., h+, OH) for florfenicol degradation and b) radicals (such as OH) can also be electrochemically generated by the nanomaterials coated on the cathode for florfenicol degradation (Fig. 5b).

Fig. 6 summarizes the different morphologies of the carbonaceous material-modified membranes based on recent literature. The membrane structures and morphologies were highly dependent on the synthesis procedures and materials. For instance, the composition of the casting solution and nanomaterial types can affect the ion exchange rate during the phase inversion process, resulting in different morphologies. Nonetheless, carbonaceous materials normally have abundant functional groups, high specific surface area, and high porosity. Hence, these membrane structures normally display a high porosity, high water permeability, and high hydrophilia, which can improve the efficiency of the membrane for the treatment of organic pollutants. Furthermore, Table 1 represents the latest reports on the fabrication of carbonaceous modified membranes for (waste)water treatment applications.

In conclusion, carbonaceous materials, as green, inexpensive, and sustainable materials, can play an important role in membrane modification for efficient (waste)water treatment. Although the prices of carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon quantum dots are slightly high, some novel and advanced technologies have been applied in recent years, which can greatly reduce the costs of these types of membranes, resulting in rapid commercialization in real wastewater treatment.

Clay is among the most available minerals on the Earth's surface [148]. There are natural resources for this mineral, such as shale rocks [149]. Bentonite, kaolin, and zeolite are well-known clay-based materials (CBMs), which have been extracted and used for several applications, such as water treatment and catalysis [150,151]. This section aims to explore and discuss the applicability of clay-based minerals for the fabrication of efficient and low-cost membrane structures and their effects on membrane performance (Fig. 7a).

Temperature can also play an important role in determining the properties of clay-modified membranes because the membrane structure is quite different under different sintering temperatures. For instance, Bouazizi et al. [155] fabricated a membrane from natural bentonite at 950 °C. The results indicated that the membrane has a homogeneous structure without any macro defects due to the recrystallization and growth of the particles under elevated temperatures, which results in a decrease in the average pore volume of the membrane. In fact, at high sintering temperatures, the densification process occurs, resulting in a decrease in porosity and an increase in bulk density. The permeability and mechanical strength also have a strong relationship with the porosity and bulk density. Hence, changes in the permeability and mechanical properties of the membrane can be expected by changing the sintering temperature.

There is evidence in the literature stating that clay dosage and membrane thickness are of high importance for the membrane to achieve efficient separation. As is well known, the dosage of clay will affect the thickness of the membrane and then affect the performance of the membrane in (waste)water treatment. For instance, Saja et al. [156] fabricated a ceramic perlite-supported bentonite membrane through a sintering method at 500 °C for 2 h. When the bentonite content increased from 0.25 to 1.50 wt%, the water permeability of the membrane increased from 60 to 2 L/(hm2 bar), and the pore size decreased from 17 to 4 nm. This result can be attributed to the membrane becoming thicker and denser with increasing bentonite content. It was also confirmed that the thickness of the membrane increased from 4 to 10 μm with increasing bentonite content from 0.25 to 1.50 wt%, and the best performance membrane was obtained at a bentonite content of 0.75 wt%. In this regard, the dosage of clay and thickness of the membrane play important roles in the higher performance of the membrane.

Bentonite is a representative aluminum phyllosilicate clay material consisting mostly of montmorillonite, which is made up of an octahedral alumina layer between two tetrahedral silica sheets. As shown in Fig. 7b, the lamellar structure contains many cations, such as Cu2+, Mg2+, Na+, and K+, which can be easily replaced by other cations. Hence, bentonite with a high specific surface area and porous structure can exhibit excellent adsorption performance for cationic contaminants through a cationic exchange mechanism [157]. Additionally, because bentonite is a widely available and inexpensive material, it can be considered a viable alternative for real-scale water and (waste)water treatment applications [158,159].

Recently, bentonite has been used for the fabrication and modification of highly efficient membrane structures for (waste)water treatment. For instance, Bouazizi et al. [160] fabricated a new flat ceramic microfiltration membrane from natural bentonite via a uniaxial pressing method. The presence of Al and Si in the structure of the precursor allowed the formation of several surface functional groups, such as SiO, AlO, and –OH, which can play an important role in the rejection of pollutants. This can be associated with the promoted adsorption of pollutants via mechanisms such as hydrogen bonding mediated by such functional groups [161]. Furthermore, Bouazizi et al. [160] also found that the membrane demonstrates a high porosity of 32.12 %, with a pore size and thickness of 1.70 μm and 1.6 mm, respectively. The most important property of the membrane for (waste)water treatment was its considerable water permeability of 520 L/(hm2 bar) and mechanical properties of 22 MPa. The performance of the membrane was tested for treating industrial effluent, and the membrane displayed excellent retention of suspended solids (94 %-99 %), resulting in complete discoloration of the (waste)water.

Using grafted-membrane-involved polymers is reported to improve the antifouling behavior of the membrane. For instance, Ahmad et al. [162] fabricated a bentonite-PVC membrane through a phase inversion method. Additionally, the membrane demonstrated an improved performance for oily (waste)water with a high oil rejection (97 %). The enhanced performance is mainly due to the improved membrane surface properties, such as porosity, pore density, pure water flux, and hydrophilicity [163]. Kadhom et al. [164] observed that the addition of bentonite to a polysulfide thin film composite (TFC) membrane can increase the salt rejection and water flux of the membrane, which can be ascribed to bentonite improving the hydrophilicity of the membrane by providing more space for water to transfer. In this regard, the authors suggested that the adsorption of water in clay interlayers can be divided into three steps. First, the initial uptake in the intermediate layer is carried out under low relative pressure, and then water molecular layers are generated as the relative pressure increases; finally, two molecular layers are built at higher relative pressure [165]. Hence, the combination of polymers and bentonite can be considered an important research direction for designing a high-performance membrane with a desired structure and high permeability.

In addition, the application of bentonite-based membranes for the adsorption of various organic and inorganic compounds from effluents has attracted much interest due to the low operating cost and advanced physicochemical properties of these types of membrane structures. As an example, Zhou et al. [166] synthesized carboxymethylated chitosan/Na-bentonite composite membranes for the adsorption of uranium(VI) from nuclear industry effluents. The membrane with a surface area of 5.48 m2/g and pore volume of 0.064 cm3/g demonstrated a homogeneous morphology, which can be attributed to the hydrophilic characteristics of bentonite that promote the miscibility of the casting solution. The membrane displayed a higher adsorption capacity (91 mg/g) than the pristine carboxymethylated chitosan membrane (80 mg/g) and Na-bentonite membrane (17.5 mg/g), mainly because bentonite can enhance the porosity of the membrane. Moreover, the adsorption capacity of the membrane decreased 11.7 % (to ~80 mg/g) after 5 cycles, indicating the potential of the membrane to be used for real (waste)water treatment due to its reusability and high performance.

Kaolinite is the most abundant hydrous aluminosilicate mineral present in soils and sediments. It has a typical layered silicate structure comprising a double-layer structure of a tetrahedral silica (SiO4) layer and an octahedral alumina [AlO2(OH)4] layer (Fig. 7c). Kaolinite generally has low plasticity, high refractory properties, a large specific surface area, and good hydrophilic performance and rheological properties, which is beneficial for a wide range of applications in ceramics, plastics, coatings, etc. [167,168]. Such properties can also make kaolinite an attractive alternative material for the preparation of membrane structures for water and (waste)water treatment. According to Bousbih et al. [169], natural kaolinite could be combined with methocel (binder) and starch (porous agent) to prepare a membrane using a sintering method at 1000 °C for 3 h. The membrane demonstrated good permeability of 21.2 L/m2·h with an average pore size of 35 nm, revealing that the membrane can be used in ultrafiltration processes. The filtration of raw effluent and biologically pretreated effluent resulted in complete decolorization (99 %, 100 %) and a high chemical oxygen demand (80 % for raw effluent, 93 % for biologically pretreated effluent) and turbidity (98 % for raw effluent, 100 % for biologically pretreated effluent) removal rate.

Nonetheless, the performance of kaolinite membranes needs to be further improved, and element doping has proven to be an effective method for improving membrane performance. For instance, Chen et al. [170] fabricated a porous inorganic membrane from lanthanum-doped kaolinite at a sintering temperature of 1150 °C. The results demonstrated that the 11 wt% La-doped kaolinite membrane has higher mechanical strength (2.5 times) and permeability (3 times) than the pristine kaolinite membrane. La doping can destroy the random directional stacking of kaolinite flakes and increase the particle contact in the sintering process. Consequently, “pot-holes” are generated to provide a bimodal porous structure, thereby reducing tortuosity and improving pore network connectivity, which facilitates an increase in membrane permeability [171]. Since the reaction causes an increase in the surface area between particles, the consolidation of the particles is promoted, and the mechanical strength is enhanced.

According to the recent literature, the percent of kaolinite can significantly influence the properties of the membranes. Belgada et al. [172] studied the influence of different kaolinite contents (5-15 wt%) on the properties of phosphate/kaolinite microfiltration membranes. The membrane with the optimized properties was obtained with 15 wt% kaolinite and sintered at 1000 °C for 2 h. The membrane displayed 41 % porosity and a high permeability of 1045 L/hm2 bar, resulting in excellent performance for textile (waste)water with 99 % turbidity removal. Herein, kaolinite can remarkably improve the porosity and permeability of the membrane, which may be due to the laminar structure formed due to the prevailed mullitization of kaolinite during the sintering process at high temperatures [173]. However, the effects of higher percentages of kaolinite on the properties of the membrane have been discussed in other studies. Hatimi et al. [174] prepared pyrrhotite ash/red kaolinite clay microfiltration membranes with different kaolinite contents of 50 %, 60 %, and 70 %. The results revealed that the membrane prepared with 50 % kaolinite (sintered at 1000 °C) can display high performance for the removal of turbidity, organic oil, and fat (100 % and 94 %, respectively) from dairy (waste) water. This high efficiency can be attributed to the high porosity (34 %) and water permeability (22.88 10−7 m3/h m2 kPa) of the membrane. During the sintering process, decomposition of the limonite to calcite, pyrite, and pyrrhotite occurs. In addition, kaolinite is transformed into mullite at high temperatures. Such processes can increase the porosity because the gases (e.g., CO2 and SO2) released during the decomposition of kaolinite promote the formation of pores [175].

The application of a mixture of kaolinite with other low-cost materials has also been considered an efficient strategy to control the properties of kaolinite-based membranes. Malik et al. [176] prepared ceramic microfiltration membranes by using a mixture of kaolin, fly ash, and dolomite. The membrane fabricated through a sintering method at 900 °C displayed an excellent porosity (>45 %) and small pore size, which can be ascribed to the effect of dolomite (a pore-forming agent). It has also been shown that dolomite can effectively inhibit the densification of coal fly ash due to the formation of pores during the decomposition of dolomite to offset the volume shrinkage caused by a sintering process. This can potentially increase the porosity of the materials [177].

Kaolinite can also be combined with other low-cost materials to fabricate membranes with enhanced properties. Zou et al. [178] developed a ceramic membrane using alumina particle-modified fly ash as the cheap support material and kaolinite as the membrane layer through a one-step co-sintering process at 1250 °C for 2 h. The membrane with an average pore size of 320 nm and water permeance of 3650 L/m2 h bar displayed a high oil rejection rate of 98.5 % for 400 ppm oil-water emulsions, which can be ascribed to the super-hydrophilic property of kaolinite and the electric repulsion between the kaolinite layer and oil droplets. They found that the presence of alumina particles can increase the melting point of the composite, which means that fly ash and kaolinite do not normally shrink at high temperatures. Additionally, alumina particles can act as an inhibitor to prevent excessive migration, which could prevent the shrinkage of the fly ash structure. Hence, a homogenous porous structure is obtained. Therefore, the composition of raw materials is an important factor for high-performance membranes when kaolinite is used to prepare kaolinite-based membranes with other substances. More studies on the application of other low-cost materials in this regard are highly welcome.

Zeolite is a hydrated aluminosilicate compound with a microporous three-dimensional structure consisting of linked TO4 tetrahedra (T = Si, Al) (Fig. 7d). The general formula of zeolite can be written as Mx/n[(AlO2)x(SiO2)y].zH2O, in which M is an extra framework cation, such as K+, Na+, and Ca2+ [179]. Zeolite has attracted considerable interest due to its excellent properties, such as permeability, adsorption properties, high exchange capacity, and thermal stability, which can make it a suitable option for the treatment of (waste)waters, gas purification, soil amendments, etc. [180,181]. Furthermore, natural zeolite, as a sustainable and inexpensive material, has been applied for the preparation of membrane structures for (waste)water treatment. Adam et al. [182] constructed a hollow fiber ceramic membrane from natural zeolite by a facile phase inversion and sintering route. The characterization confirmed the asymmetric structure of the membrane. Additionally, the sintering temperature did not affect the surface functional groups present in the membrane structure but influenced the grain size and porosity. A less porous structure with tightly packed grains is formed as the sintering temperature increases (above 1000 °C), and when the temperature is higher than 1150 °C, the grains are fused with the formation of defect pores. The membrane displayed a high mechanical strength of 50.92 MPa and a water permeability of 249.57 L/hm2 bar at a sintering temperature of 1050 °C with excellent performance for the removal of ammonia (90 %), which can be attributed to the effect of the porous structure, the decrease in permeation rate, and the dehydration of OH groups.

Despite the applicability of zeolites for the preparation of membrane structures, modifications may be required to enhance their performance. For instance, the addition of secondary elements to the structure of the membrane can potentially increase the porosity and hence the efficiency of the system for the treatment of effluents. Aloulou et al. [183] prepared an ultrafiltration membrane using clay-TiO2 nanoparticles as the surface layer and a zeolite tube as the support. The nanoparticles were obtained through a colloidal route, and the membrane was fabricated by using a layer-by-layer fabrication method, followed by a sintering process at 900 °C for 3 h. The membrane displayed a homogenous structure with an average pore diameter of 18 nm. This can be attributed to the nanoparticle as a precursor of membrane formulation, which can provide a special pore size distribution in membrane preparation [184]. The membrane with a water permeability of 80 L/hm2.bar exhibited a high turbidity rejection rate (>90 %) for treating textile industrial effluents, washing water, effluents containing heavy metals from the electroplating industry, and agro-food (waste)water. Additionally, the process exhibited an acceptable COD removal of 87.4 % as well as 95 % color retention for industrial (waste)water. It is worth mentioning here that the membrane could be recovered using a cleaning procedure. The reused membrane also demonstrated a high water flux of 77 L/hm2.bar. For the cleaning process, a solution of NaOH (2 %) was applied at 80 °C for 20 min, followed by rinsing with deionized water until reaching neutral pH. Subsequently, a nitric acid (2 %) solution was used at 60 °C for 20 min, followed by deionized water cleaning until reaching neutral pH. In fact, NaOH can remove proteins, polysaccharides, suspended impurities, and other pollutants by loosening, emulsifying, and dispersing sediments on the membrane surface. Furthermore, HNO3 can significantly remove the sediments produced by calcium, magnesium, iron, and other metal ions [185].

It has also been documented in the literature that the application of supports for the fabrication of zeolite membranes can reduce the mass transfer resistance and improve the water flux. This is mainly because the addition of a porous support can improve the pore size and porosity of the membrane [186]. Wang et al. [187] synthesized NaA zeolite membranes on coarse macroporous α-Al2O3 tubes through a microwave-assisted heating method. The results revealed that the membrane with high permeability and stability displayed an almost complete NaCl rejection efficiency of simulated seawater. It was found that ball-milled NaA zeolite can be considered small crystal seeds and that the microwave-assisted heating method can improve the nucleation rate and crystal growth. The nucleation rate and crystal growth can be ascribed to the adjustment of the activation energy barriers, and microwave heating is a rapid method to achieve the required activation energy, so the nucleation process is fast and uniform, which can effectively avoid the agglomeration of materials. In addition, the rapid growth of small crystal seeds can promote the formation of materials with a high specific surface area during the microwave-assisted synthesis process [188]. The development and application of such facile and green techniques for the fabrication of support materials can be highly recommended for future studies to fabricate cost-effective and efficient membrane structures to deal with industrial and nonindustrial effluents.

There are also other types of clay-based materials, such as talc, halloysite, and palygorskite, which can be used for the modification of membrane structures. Talc is a white hydrated magnesium silicate with the chemical formula Mg3Si4O10(OH)2 [189]. The surface of the talc crystal is composed of a basal surface and edge surface, and the edge face is hydrophilic due to the SiOH and MgOH groups, while the basal surface does not contain a hydrophilic group [190]. Moradihamedani et al. [191] prepared a polysulfone/Fe3O4-talc membrane by using a phase inversion technique. The polysulfone/Fe3O4-talc membrane displayed higher lead and nickel ion rejection (99.4 % and 96.2 %, respectively) than the polysulfone/talc membrane (90.0 % and 86.1 %, respectively), which can be attributed to the higher surface area of Fe3O4-talc (37.079 m2/g) than talc (6.675 m2/g). The hydrophilic groups of the edge face could be changed into negatively charged particles (such as ≡SiO−) during the reaction process, so the Fe3O4-talc of the membrane can adsorb the heavy metal ions due to the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged metal ion and negatively charged particles (such as ≡SiO−).

Halloysite is another layered silicoaluminate (1:1) clay mineral in which the octahedral alumina layer alternates with the tetrahedral silica layer. Halloysite has a special hollow tubular morphology due to the difference in the arrangement of the two layers when tetrahedral silica sheets are combined with octahedral alumina sheets. Halloysite has attracted much interest due to its excellent properties, such as high surface area and little or no toxicity to living organisms [192]. As an example, Qin et al. [193] fabricated porous polyacrylonitrile-supported halloysite nanotube membranes using an evaporation-induced method. The membrane demonstrated an excellent rejection efficiency for reactive black 5 (97.7 %) with high salt permeation (86.5 %). Furthermore, after coating halloysite on a polysulfone substrate, the membrane displayed an increased water flux, and the irreversible fouling decreased considerably. This can be attributed to the fact that halloysite can increase the surface hydrophilicity and decrease the surface roughness, which effectively contributes to the prevention of membrane fouling [194].

Palygorskite is a natural nanorod-shaped hydrated magnesium aluminosilicate mineral with a monoclinic crystal microstructure (1-5 μm), which, together with the specific shape, grants a large specific surface area to the materials. In comparison with other types of clay-based materials, it exhibits excellent salt resistance (which is defined as insensitivity to salt water [195]) and a rapid hydration rate, which make it an attractive candidate for (waste)water treatment applications as an outstanding adsorbent [196]. Due to its inherent properties, palygorskite can also be used to prepare a membrane for the filtration of effluents. Wei et al. [197] fabricated a polyvinylidene fluoride/palygorskite composite ultrafiltration membrane by using two techniques: a) nonsolvent-induced phase separation and b) a thermally induced phase separation process. They discussed the influence of palygorskite particle size on the membrane properties, including porosity and rejection potential. Membranes with a small size of palygorskite (302 nm) demonstrated higher porosity (80 % > 79 %), lower thickness (98 < 101 μm), and lower surface roughness (29 nm < 33 nm) than membranes with a large size of palygorskite (450 nm). This can be ascribed to the fact that palygorskite can promote the crystallization process of polyvinylidene fluoride (due to the increased concentration in the unit space) and increase the rate of solvent/nonsolvent exchange, as has also been observed in other studies [198]. However, other studies have discussed that membranes made of larger particle size materials have low porosity and higher mechanical strength due to the strong binding force between palygorskite and the polymer [199]. Hence, it can be concluded that membranes fabricated with relatively small particles can display high rejection and high antifouling properties due to their high porosity and low surface roughness [200].

Table 2 summarizes the findings of the latest reports on the fabrication of clay-modified membranes for (waste)water treatment applications. In this table, supporting information regarding the various synthesis methods applied for the fabrication of membrane structures and the resulting critical membrane properties, including thickness, pore size, permeability of the membrane, and rejection efficiency of pollutants, are listed. It is evident from this table that spin coating and dip coating are the most widely used methods for the fabrication of membrane structures. It can be concluded that various types of clay-based membranes have been widely used for the treatment of oil and dye wastewater. These membranes normally demonstrate an excellent rejection efficiency of over 90 %, but the literature is still poor for information regarding the application of such membrane structures for the treatment of contaminants of emerging concern, such as pharmaceutically active compounds or salts in wastewaters. Hence, there is a need to develop advanced and sustainable techniques for the fabrication of clay-based nanofiltration or reverse osmosis membranes. In conclusion, it can be stated that clay-based materials have broad application prospects in wastewater treatment mainly because they are abundant and low-cost natural feedstocks.

Organic polymers such as poly(vinylidene fluoride), polysulfone, and polyvinylidene have been conventionally used for the fabrication of membranes. However, the gradual reduction in fossil resources, together with the increasing concern about the effects of the release of greenhouse gases (GHGs) into the atmosphere from the consumption of traditional fossil fuels (such as global warming and climate change), has increased research interest in the fabrication of membranes from natural polymers. It is well known that natural polymers, including polysaccharides, proteins, peptides and polyesters, are generally biocompatible and contain various functional groups [215]. Hence, as illustrated in Fig. 8 , this section focuses on the applicability of natural polymers in the fabrication of membrane structures and their application in (waste)water treatment.

Cellulose, as a prevalent polysaccharide, has some inherent advantages, such as being abundant, biocompatible, eco-friendly, and highly hydrophilic (with a water contact angle in the range of 20-30°) [216,217]. Cellulose-modified membranes normally present excellent antifouling performance due to their outstanding hydrophilicity. Additionally, such membranes can demonstrate high degrees of stability over a wide range of pH values due to the strong interactions of hydrogen bonds with cellulose chains [218]. Hu et al. [219] fabricated a cellulose microfiltration membrane through a thermally induced phase separation process by using a lower critical solution temperature system. They observed an increase in the water flux of the cellulose membranes from 31 L/m2·h to 211 L/m2·h as the coagulation temperature increased from 10 °C to 50 °C. This result suggests that the higher temperature can result in the formation of pores, as well as loose structures, which are both beneficial to enhance the water flux of the membrane. In fact, the phase separation of the cellulose casting solution occurs quickly since heat transfer is the major driving force under elevated temperatures, while phase separation is controlled by nonsolvent-induced phase separation at low temperatures. Because heat transfer is significantly faster than mass transfer, the longer time for phase separation leads to the regeneration of hydrogen bonds in cellulose at low temperatures, resulting in the formation of dense membrane structures. Moreover, the cellulose membrane exhibited a high rejection efficiency of 99 % for peanut oil and pump oil (waste)water nanoemulsions.

Chitin is another abundant polysaccharide that is mainly extracted from seafood wastes. It is one of the most abundant natural polymers in the oceans, and it has unique properties, such as intrinsic biocompatibility and good biodegradability, which make it among the most appropriate natural polymers for the fabrication of membrane structures [220]. For instance, Wang et al. [221] fabricated a chitin nanofibrous membrane from partially deacetylated chitin nanofibers with abundant amino functional groups. The membrane exhibited an excellent oil rejection of 99 % for chloroform, mainly because of its super-hydrophilicity and underwater super-oleophobic surface, as well as the small pore size of <48 nm. Furthermore, the membrane demonstrated a high adsorption capacity for various noble metal ions (243, 137, 218, and 198 mg/g for Au3+, Ag+, Pt4+, and Pd2+ ions, respectively), resulting from the chelation between the surface amino groups of chitin and noble metal ions. Surprisingly, the adsorbed noble metal ions on the surface of the membrane can be autonomously reduced in situ into noble metal ion particles without any other solvents. This can be attributed to the fact that the abundant amino functional groups of the membrane can act as a reducing agent for noble metal ions. Obviously, the integration of oil separation and the reduction of noble metal ions would be an advantage toward sustainable (waste)water treatment.

Chitosan is a derivative of chitin and is also well known for its abundance of surface functional groups (e.g., -OH, -NH2). Hence, chitosan-based membranes can also exhibit hydrophilic properties and improve antifouling performance when used for the treatment of polluted (waste)waters [222]. Long et al. [223] developed a facile multistep coating process to fabricate chitosan nanofiltration membranes (Fig. 9a-b). They found that the thickness of the membrane increased slightly with repeating the casting procedure (from 1 to 4 times). In addition, the water flux of the membrane decreased rapidly as the casting times increased, which can be attributed to the fact that the thicker membrane increases the resistance of water passing through them. However, the thicker membrane demonstrated a high rejection efficiency for (waste)water treatment. The membrane prepared under four-stage casting had a molecular size cut-off of 5 Å and developed pore structures (Fig. 9c), and it also showed a high rejection efficiency of >99 % for brilliant blue (Fig. 9d). Furthermore, they observed that the water flux, dye rejection efficiency, and surface structure were the same between the pristine membrane and rinsing membrane (0.1 M NaOH solution for 30 mins), suggesting that the chitosan membrane is stable and has excellent antifouling properties.

In addition to the type of cellulose, chitin, and chitosan used for the fabrication of the membranes, other natural polymers can be used in the fabrication of the membrane structures. Starch is among the inexpensive, environmentally friendly, and widely used porous agents that have been used in the structure of membranes. It can generate pores during thermal treatment at temperatures above 300 °C [224].
