Changing the dosage of starch can influence the physicochemical properties of the ceramic membrane, including porosity, mechanical strength, and water permeability. For instance, Samhari et al. [225] prepared a microfiltration membrane from Moroccan natural kaolinite clay using corn starch as a porosity agent. The kaolinite microfiltration membrane was fabricated using a uniaxial pressing method and sintering at 1100 °C. The membrane with an average pore size of 2.3 μm displayed excellent turbidity rejection for raw seawater and agro-food effluent (73 %, 99 %). The authors also studied the effects of different dosages of starch on the properties of the membrane. The average pore size of the membrane increased from 1.9 to 3.4 μm when the amount of starch increased from 0 to 15 %, which can be attributed to open spaces created by starch resulting in larger pores and higher porosity during the sintering process. However, the mechanical strength of the membrane decreased from 23.3 ± 0.6 to 18.4 ± 0.5 MPa when the amount of starch increased from 0 to 15 %. This can clearly demonstrate a reduction in mechanical strength due to the increase in the porosity and the pore volume of the membrane.

Alginate is another natural polymer that is usually extracted from brown algae. In general, alginate has interesting properties, such as good biocompatibility and biodegradability, nontoxicity, availability, high viscosity, and the ability to gel with multivalent cations, which make it an ideal candidate for membrane fabrication in (waste)water treatment [226]. There are reports investigating the effects of the alginate content on the overall performance of the membrane. For instance, Li et al. [227] fabricated a porous alginate membrane through a novel vacuum freeze-drying process. They found that the membrane with 1 % sodium alginate displayed an abundant porous structure, which is of high interest for water filtration applications. The porous structure was further developed by increasing the content of sodium alginate to 5 %. This can be attributed to the fact that more ice in the membrane structure is sublimated at the high percentage of sodium alginate during the freeze-drying process, which can result in a more developed pore membrane structure. Moreover, the membrane with 1 % sodium alginate demonstrated a relatively high rejection efficiency of 85 % for MB, but the membrane with 5 % sodium alginate showed only 51 % rejection for MB. This phenomenon can be explained by the developed porous structure of the membrane allowing MB molecules to pass through the membrane pores. However, the membrane with 5 % sodium alginate presented a slightly higher adsorption capacity for MB than the membrane with 1 % sodium alginate (560 mg/g, 545 mg/g), which can be related to the fact that the developed porous structure is beneficial for MB adsorption [228]. In conclusion, the abundance of pores can favor adsorption. However, excessive porosity may lead to a decrease in the pollutant rejection efficiency of the membrane. Although natural polymers are green, sustainable feedstocks for the fabrication of membranes, most commercial applications use synthetic polymers. Hence, research on the application of natural polymers in the fabrication of membranes for (waste)water treatment is currently of high interest in the literature.

In addition to carbonaceous materials, clay-based materials, and natural polymers, discussed in the previous sections, there are also some other low-cost materials, such as perlite and magnesite, which have been utilized (but not extensively) for the fabrication of membrane structures with high efficiencies for (waste)water treatment applications. Hence, the main purpose of this section is to discuss the applicability of such materials for the preparation of novel membrane structures for efficient (waste)water treatment applications.

Perlite, as a material with a wide range of applications (e.g., in the building industry [229]), is characterized as an amorphous glass-based structure including approximately 75 % silicon oxide (SiO2), usually formed by the hydration of obsidian (as a naturally occurring volcanic glass [230]). Perlite is softened by applying a thermal treatment at temperatures above 850 °C, resulting in evaporation of the water that exists in the perlite structure. As a result, perlite expands rapidly during heat treatment, leading to the formation of pores, which is of high importance for water filtration applications [231]. Saja et al. [232] fabricated a microfiltration membrane from natural perlite through a uniaxial pressing and sintering process at 950 °C. The membrane with a porosity of 52.11 % and an average pore size of 1.70 μm displayed a high water permeability of 1433 L/hm2 bar, which can be attributed to the alveolar texture and sandy property of the perlite, as the raw material used for the fabrication of the membrane. Furthermore, the membrane displayed excellent chemical resistance and a high turbidity removal rate for agrofood and tannery effluents (97 % and 96 %, respectively).

Magnesite is also an abundant carbonate hydrophilic mineral ([233]), with a wide range of applications in various fields, such as alloy manufacturing, aerospace, catalysis, and the food industry [234]. It has also been used as the main feedstock for the synthesis of magnesium oxide. Regarding the physicochemical properties, magnesite can display a high corrosion resistance [235]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) also revealed that raw magnesite displays a major weight loss of ~32 wt% from 400 °C to 600 °C due to the decomposition of magnesium carbonate. However, only a small weight loss of ~2 wt% can be observed at 670-760 °C, which can be associated with the decomposition of calcium carbonate at these temperatures [236]. This result demonstrates the high thermal stability of magnesite, especially at elevated temperatures. Such properties can make magnesite an appropriate candidate for the fabrication of stable membrane structures for the treatment of real effluents under harsh operating conditions. Such an approach has been reported for the fabrication of low-cost ceramic microfiltration membranes, as by Manni et al. [237]. The porosity and water permeability of the prepared magnesite membrane decreased from 51 % to 35 % and 2107 to 440 L/hm2bar as the sintering temperature increased from 900 to 1200 °C, respectively. This can be explained by the partial densification of ceramic materials at high temperatures [238]. However, the bulk density and mechanical strength increased by elevating the sintering temperature. Hence, the membrane with optimum properties was obtained at 1100 °C and demonstrated a high porosity of 48 % and a considerable water permeability of 922 L/hm2bar. The membrane with a pore size of 1.12 μm also showed a high turbidity rejection of 99.9 % for the textile effluents.

Wood has also been considered an interesting material for filtration applications because of its abundance and mesoporous structure containing partially aligned nanochannels, as well as plenty of hydroxyl groups [239]. For instance, Chen et al. [43] fabricated a palladium-decorated wood membrane for efficient MB removal. The mesoporous membrane with abundant surface hydroxyl groups demonstrated a high permeability of 1 × 105 L·m–2·h–1 and an excellent MB removal efficiency (>99.8 %). This can be explained by the following: 1) the presence of hydroxyl groups that can help immobilize Pd nanoparticles; 2) the partially aligned channels as well as nanochannels in the wood structure, which leads to an increase in the likelihood of contact between MB and Pd nanoparticles; and 3) the presence of long channels decorated with Pd nanoparticles that facilitate the bulk treatment of (waste)water. The proposed cost-effective and high-performance wood membrane is a promising material for real (waste)water treatment.

In summary, carbonaceous materials, clay-based materials, and natural polymers can be used to modify and improve the properties of conventional membranes. However, some points can be highlighted and concluded while making decisions for the modification of the existing membranes. Carbonaceous-modified membranes normally exhibit high chemical resistance, high mechanical strength, and excellent separation performance for a wide range of organic and inorganic pollutants. Such membranes are also easy to tailor because of the unique properties of the carbonaceous materials added (e.g., appropriate surface charge, high specific surface area and abundance of surface functional groups). Clay-based membranes can also exhibit high thermal stability and cost-effectiveness, while they have some drawbacks, such as poor hydrophilicity and easy fouling. Clay-based membranes are also relatively sensitive to harsh operating conditions (e.g., strong acidic environments) due to the possibility of leaching metal ions from such structures. Finally, polymer-based membranes can be considered environmentally friendly membranes because natural polymers are normally renewable and biodegradable. Hence, they generally have high biocompatibility and hydrophilicity, while their stability and mechanical strength need to be improved.

Although using naturally derived materials to improve the membrane properties in wastewater treatment applications has attracted the interest of researchers, there are still some important issues to address in future studies. This section aims to discuss the existing challenges and opportunities for the fabrication and modification of membranes by naturally derived materials for the treatment of effluents.

At present, membrane technology has been researched extensively for wastewater treatment, while there are some aspects that have restricted their wider use for real wastewater treatment applications. To simultaneously maximize the porosity of the membrane and its rejection efficiency. Notably, there is always a trade-off between the water permeability and rejection efficiency of the membrane structures. The excellent water permeability of the membrane is highly dependent on the large pore size, but the large pore size can allow the passage of pollutants. To overcome this issue, the present manuscript calls for studies that utilize appropriate techniques for creating pores (such as ultrasonic irradiation) [240] while incorporating appropriate and low-cost materials into the membrane structure to enhance the hydrophilicity of the membrane.

Membrane fouling is still a challenging issue for wastewater treatment applications. Although the decoration of the membrane with the materials reviewed in this manuscript can enhance the antifouling performance of the membranes, the mechanisms involved in their antifouling properties have not yet been fully investigated and understood. This is especially of high importance for the next generations of membranes designed for the simultaneous rejection and oxidation of pollutants. In this regard, advanced in situ and real-time characterization technologies (such as FTIR and SEM) are required to trace the behavior of the membrane when it is being used for the treatment of polluted (waste)waters. However, the stability of nanomaterial-decorated membranes is also an important parameter determining their applicability for real wastewater treatment. The leaching of nanomaterials from the membrane surface can cause secondary environmental pollution, requiring an efficient post-treatment or recycling step to avoid their release into the environment. Hence, exploring methods to enhance the stability of the membrane and the coating layer has been of high interest to the scientific community. In general, the immobilization of nanomaterials into membrane structures can be achieved either by physical adsorption or chemical interaction processes. Physical adsorption is mainly controlled by the van der Waals force or electrostatic interaction between the membrane structure and the nanomaterials. Considering that this force is relatively weak, leaching of the nanomaterials can be expected to form membrane structures. The chemical immobilization of nanomaterials is another method of their inclusion into membrane structures. The creation of chemical bonds or the establishment of cross-links is the main mechanism involved in such processes. Hence, much stronger bonding is established between the nanomaterials and the membranes under such mechanisms [241]. In particular, chemical bonding can provide advantages for anchoring nanomaterials into membrane structures, such as simplicity and low cost. For instance, Lei et al. [242] fabricated a TiO2-polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix membrane via solution casting combined with a post-thermal treatment. The membrane had a high degree of stability, and the release of TiO2 was negligible even after 25 cycles. This is attributed to the formation of a strong chemical bond of Ti-O-C between the TiO2 and PVA through the dehydration reaction during the heat treatment process. The uniform distribution of the nanomaterials in the membrane structure is also very important to prevent any defects in the membrane structure during the fabrication process. In this regard, techniques such as ultrasonic irradiation can be applied to improve the homogeneous distribution of the nanomaterials.

It is also worth mentioning that the technologies reviewed and discussed in this manuscript for the modification of membrane structures have been mainly developed and tested on small and lab scales. Hence, there is an urgent need to conduct pilot and large-scale studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of these technologies for real (waste)water treatment applications. In this regard, simplified and automatic preparation approaches for membranes are necessary for commercial applications. Furthermore, it is recommended that future studies consider sustainability aspects for the fabrication of membrane structures (see [243]) while considering the optimization of the properties of the membranes with the naturally derived materials discussed in the present review. This is also recommended for future studies to run continuous and long-term membrane filtration processes using the technologies discussed in the present manuscript, especially with real effluents under various operating conditions, to simulate the harsh conditions of large-scale wastewater treatment processes.

In addition, the outcome of these studies clearly demonstrated that carbonaceous materials, clay-based materials, and natural polymers can considerably contribute to the enhancement of the properties of membrane structures and push their commercialization for real applications. However, there is still a need to explore other types of low-cost materials for further improvement of the membrane properties. As discussed before, the properties of carbonaceous materials, clay, and natural polymers are highly dependent on the feedstocks, which can affect the membrane performance in wastewater treatment applications. In this regard, the specifications and quality checks of the raw materials need to be considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Economic considerations and regional development of membrane technology
ce:section.ce:para: The costs associated with wastewater treatment processes are considered to be one of the major concerns of using these technologies worldwide. Such costs are mainly classified into initial investments, operating costs, and maintenance costs [243]. The initial investments are dependent on the wastewater treatment plant scale, and they mainly include the costs of land, civil works, equipment, etc. In addition, providing electricity, labor, and chemicals brings operating and maintenance costs. Table 3 illustrates the reported costs of different wastewater treatment technologies on both laboratory and real scales in various regions of the world. According to this table, the overall treatment costs are also dependent on the type of effluents and the applied technology. For instance, various treatment costs have been reported for chemical-based treatments such as the photo-Fenton process. Cabrera Reina et al. [244] reported that the overall costs of the treatment of industrial wastewater in Tabernas (Spain), Arica (Chile), and Doha (Qatar) using solar photo-Fenton processes were 3.24 USD/m3, 2.65 USD/m3, and 2.38 USD/m3, respectively. The difference can be attributed to the differences in the solar intensities in different countries in addition to the local prices of raw materials, land, and labor. However, higher treatment costs have been reported for highly polluted effluents and toxic compounds. As an example, Ribeiro et al. [245] estimated the operating costs for the removal of adsorbable organic halogens (AOXs) in the photo-Fenton process as 47.5 USD/m3 in Portugal. Mejri et al. [246] reported that the overall cost of the Fe3+-nitrilotriacetic acid system for the treatment of secondary effluents in the solar photo-Fenton process was 0.14 USD/m3 in Tunisia.

However, relatively low treatment costs have been reported for the treatment of effluents using membrane technologies compared to chemical-based treatments such as the photo-Fenton process, electro-Fenton process, hydrodynamic cavitation, and ozonation (Table 3). The combination of membrane technologies with other techniques (such as biological treatments) has also been reported as very efficient and cost-effective even on industrial scales [247]. There are also reports on the industrial application of membrane technologies in rapidly developing countries. For instance, in Singapore, membrane technologies are among the most commonly used methods for recycling sewage and providing water resources for industrial applications [248]. Because membrane-based technologies generally need small areas to establish, it is of high feasibility to construct them in land-scarce urban cities of such developing countries. Furthermore, the membrane systems can be updated in a short time to suit the stringent environmental regulations for the discharge of industrial and non-industrial effluents. In addition, recent improvements in the quality of the membranes resulting in their high chemical and mechanical strength have made them more attractive for the treatment of industrial effluents. However, there is still limited evidence for the application of membrane-based technologies in some developing countries, such as Ethiopia [249]. This is mainly due to the total costs (e.g., initial investments, operating, and maintenance costs) of the membrane system exceeding the affordability of consumers in these countries. Additionally, membrane treatment plants normally require precise equipment and highly automated operations with competent personnel, and this supporting equipment and experts are very limited in such developing countries. To address this issue, cooperation between developed and developing countries is paramount to ensure successful technology transfer of these membrane technologies.

Furthermore, various attempts have been made to further reduce the overall costs of membrane technologies and promote their commercialization in real wastewater treatment applications. For instance, Chen et al. [250] discussed that the operating costs of different membrane-based treatment technologies, including P1 (pre-treatment-nanofiltration-reverse osmosis), P2 (pre-treatment-membrane-bioreactor-nanofiltration/reverse osmosis), and P3 (pre-treatment-anaerobic biological process-membrane bioreactor-nanofiltration/reverse osmosis), were 4.48, 3.82, and 3.59 USD/m3, respectively. This suggests that the implementation of an efficient pre-treatment before the membrane process can considerably reduce the membrane operating costs, although it can cause additional costs regarding the implementation of the pre-treatment step.

Another possible way to decrease the overall treatment costs of membrane-based technologies is to innovate and implement strategies to reduce the operating costs, considering that the initial investments (e.g., costs of land, civil works, and equipment) are difficult to decrease. It has been reported that electricity expenditure is among the most important parameters determining the operating and maintenance costs due to membrane technologies relying on pressure to provide penetration power. Hence, developing clean and inexpensive sources of energy and innovating self-sustained treatment facilities using membranes with improved quality and performance are the key elements in the reduction of overall treatment costs. For instance, reducing the membrane area required to treat a certain volume of effluent (A(m2)/V(m3)), developing alternative membrane structures (e.g., inorganic membranes), using cheaper raw materials to overcome the rapid fouling properties of the membrane, increasing the stability and lifetime of the membrane, and reducing the required cleaning times of the membrane through the improvement of the antifouling properties. Among these, using naturally derived feedstock materials for the fabrication of the membrane can not only reduce the costs of the feedstock but also enhance the membrane properties, including water permeability and antifouling properties. It is worth mentioning that some naturally derived materials, including GO and CNTs, are normally expensive through traditional synthesis routes, but some novel synthesis techniques have been recently developed to reduce their production cost (see Sections 2.3 and 2.4). According to these discussions, it can be concluded that using naturally derived materials to improve the performance of membranes for wastewater treatment can be considered an efficient, inexpensive, and sustainable method.

Membrane technologies are playing an increasingly important role in the treatment of effluents originating from various industrial and nonindustrial sources. They can bring advantages such as high treatment efficiencies, ease of implementation, and low environmental and health subsequent impacts, which are essential for the rapid regeneration of clean water resources. Such technologies are currently used not only for the separation/rejection of pollutants from wastewater streams but also for the degradation of complex organic compounds by loading the appropriate catalytic materials into the membrane structure. Furthermore, the presence of functional materials can improve the properties of membranes, such as porosity, hydrophobicity, water permeability, rejection efficiency, etc. [260,261]. In this regard, there is an urgent need for the selection of the best materials for the modification of membrane structures toward further commercialization of such sustainable technologies. To address this need, the present manuscript aims to present and discuss the latest progress in the application of inexpensive, environmentally friendly, and efficient materials for the fabrication of membrane structures in (waste)water treatment. In addition, the manuscript has explored the effects of various operating parameters on the performance of the modified membranes. The manuscript also tends to promote the application of low-cost and environmentally friendly carbonaceous materials (such as biochar, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots), clay-based materials (such as bentonite, kaolinite, and zeolite), and natural polymers (such as cellulose, chitosan, and starch) for the fabrication and modification of efficient membrane structures. In addition, the economic analysis proved that the use of naturally derived materials to improve the membrane properties is an efficient method to reduce the overall costs of these technologies for wastewater treatment applications. Furthermore, this review calls for further studies in this regard to provide sustainable alternatives for industrial and nonindustrial applications.

Yongtao Xue: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mohammadreza Kamali: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. S.M. Al-Salem: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Barbara Rossi: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Lise Appels: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Raf Dewil: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
